worksheets.title: Sensate focus — week 1 worksheet
worksheets.intro: Sensate focus is a structured touch exercise that removes performance pressure and rebuilds embodied closeness. This worksheet walks you through one 20-minute session.

sections.heading: Before you start
bullets: Agree on a clear yes/no signal you can use any time without explanation.
No goal of intercourse or orgasm. Really.
Phones in another room. Soft light. Warm room.
Decide who is the giver and who is the receiver first. You'll swap halfway.

sections.heading: Round 1 — 8 minutes
bullets: Receiver: lie down, eyes closed, focus on sensation.
Giver: slow, curious touch on non-genital areas only — back, arms, legs, scalp.
If either of you feels pressure to escalate, pause and breathe.

sections.heading: Swap — 8 minutes
bullets: Same rules, swapped roles.
Notice without commenting. Words come later.

sections.heading: Debrief — 4 minutes
bullets: What felt good?
Where did your mind wander?
What's one thing you'd like to try again next time?

worksheets.title: Mapping your brakes and accelerators
worksheets.intro: Based on Bancroft & Janssen's dual-control model. Most low-desire situations are about brakes (what turns you off) more than missing accelerators (what turns you on). This is a private worksheet — don't share unless you want to.

sections.heading: Accelerators — what turns you on?
bullets: Specific kinds of touch:
Specific contexts (time of day, alone, partnered, mood):
Specific words, sounds, or scents:
Specific emotional states (safety, novelty, focus, playfulness):

sections.heading: Brakes — what turns you off?
bullets: Body-related (tiredness, illness, body image):
Stress-related (work, money, family):
Relationship-related (resentment, mismatched effort):
Performance-related (worry about how I'm doing):
Interpersonal (fear of judgment, history of shaming):

sections.heading: One brake to ease this week
bullets: Pick the most easeable, not the most painful.
What is one small action that would lower it 10%?

worksheets.title: Repair after a fight — a 10-minute script
worksheets.intro: Every couple ruptures. Healthy couples repair. Use this script after the heat has cooled, ideally within 24 hours.

sections.heading: Step 1 — Acknowledge the hurt (2 min)
bullets: Name what happened, not who's right. Example: 'When you raised your voice, I shut down.'
Avoid 'you always' and 'you never'.

sections.heading: Step 2 — Take a piece of responsibility (2 min)
bullets: Find one thing you contributed, however small. Example: 'I came in defensive because I was already stressed about work.'

sections.heading: Step 3 — Repair attempt (3 min)
bullets: Offer a repair. It can be tender, silly, or direct. Example: 'I love you. Let's try this one again.'
If it lands, the rupture closes. If not, that's a sign for couples therapy.

sections.heading: Step 4 — Plan for next time (3 min)
bullets: What signal will you give next time you're flooded?
What does a 20-minute break look like? (drink water, walk, return at a specific time)
